sentences.text: This also English name.
Good morning.
Edward Polidi-- P-O-L-I-D-I, Edward-- E-D-W-A-R-D.
I'm born in Sofia, Bulgaria, the year of 1935, 23rd of December, first day of the winter.
As my memoirs of stories that were said to me by my father, speak that it was extremely cold day with lot of snow.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This produced difficulties for the transportation of my mother to the hospital.
So the delivery was done in my own house.
And this was in Sophia, [INAUDIBLE] number 55, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] third floor there, apartment number eight.
The-- they told me that when I was born, I was going to be a very lucky, fortunate, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] happy, happy person.
The story goes that I was born with these circles in my head.
And I was behaving after, of course, the first few hours, as a very, very happy child.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very early in my childhood, they discerned that the things that was attracting me was the sounds, specially music sounds.

And since my mother was, in her early days, singing with the opera theater there in the choir, I had access pretty early in my life to the music of very great masters, such as Mozart, the music of Verdi, music of Rossini, Puccini, operistic music specially, and of course, some of the songs of Schubert.
Everything what was singably beautiful was on this time somewhat familiar with me.
And this how happened that in very early days of my life, I somehow got involved with music.
This was happening in my third birthday when some of the, the guests brought me some-- a little toy violin and one toy, something like xylophone.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I really didn't know how the, the violin come between my hands.
But here was something that was decide-- decides eventually for the destiny of my life later on.
I was, in the beginning as a very little child, singing some of these melodies or trying to reproduce them in the xylophone.
Because the violin was not producing sounds, this toy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was attractive only with the-- perhaps with the [INAUDIBLE] the fact that eventually, the-- something like that in the future is possible to produce sounds.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So from this early moments of my life, I started to show some preferences toward music.
And I was singing obviously in tune enough to carry a song from beginning until the end.

And this perhaps helped that when I reached the age almost of entering in a preschool class, preschool group, I, I was already able to, to sing a number of songs in, in a way in which they were able-- my, my parents were able to prove that their little child have a musical ear, which made them very happy at that time.
Tell me about your parents.
My father was a teacher in his early ages.
His name was Israel Polidi.
He was born in Ruse.
This was one of the oldest cities in Bulgaria and the time was kind of a spiritual center for Bulgaria.
It was located on, on the river, Danube.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the reason why I was serving-- was so highly developed culturally was because was kind of connection between the culture of two countries, Bulgaria and Romania.
Matter of fact, the city was closer to Bucharest, which was the capital of Romania than to Sofia.
It was the capital of Bulgaria.
So was crossroad of number of cultures that were going in, in the town.
They were speaking in the time more than eight, nine languages at the time currently in, in the street.
For this period of Ruse speaks very much my uncle, who, was also born there, in his book, What was his name?
His name was Elias Canetti.
He was the Nobel Prize winner of 1981.
The relation was in-- from my mother's side.
And I think that is something like [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] generation of his grandmother and my aunts or my grandmother sides connected.
Of course, he left Ruse very early in his own childhood, when he was five or six years old, and never, never came back there.
You
My father also left Ruse.
And actually, I think that he met my wife already in the capital.
Maybe they knew each other very little.
But they were very young in their native city, which was the same for my mother and father.
My mother also was born in Ruse.
Her name was Zelma Cohen.
She was born-- she was considerably younger to him.
She was born something-- somewhere around '11 or '12 of 19-- 1912, I guess.

So [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was born in Sofia in one environment that was, in the time, I'm able to say, pretty happy, pretty well settled, was one world, world of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] economic and financial security in which the parents were making plans for the future of their children, of their grandchildren, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was one world of [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] quite active cultural life, quite active social life.
Can you describe that for me a little bit more?
Well, the synagogue of Sofia was one of the biggest and more beautiful in the Balkans.
And the, the life of the Jewish people was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pretty much connected with this synagogue.
They-- inside of the synagogue, they, they used to have school, a very big school, starting from the preschool-- very, very well organized.
And this is where, actually, I started my very early learning, studying, studying of the Hebrew language also.
Of course, later on, when I went to Israel, was absolutely forgotten everything there.
But never less, the life there was pretty, pretty rich.
The, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the Jewish families were visiting each other was where I, I remember how often my mother used to be visited of my aunts, my uncles, as well as other relatives.
And so many times, she would prepare [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] her visits for these relatives also.
Well, she used to dress me a little bit neater, a little prettier.
I used to be prepared to sing some song or play something on my little xylophone.
Or I had also one mouth harmonica.
And I had also one little this musical instrument, what is like vertical flutes.
They call in Hebrew [HEBREW].
Here is--

Recorder-- yeah, one recorder, to which I was improvising some of the songs that I have learned, some of which were based in the music of the greatest masters, like famous songs of operas of Mozart, some songs of Schubert, of course, in children translation, child translation.
I remember how this used-- not only how it used to make her happy, but how this used to also astonish and make a very strong impression, impressions on the relatives and cousins.
It was expressing their satisfaction and their enthusiasm of my musical activities there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was, unfortunately, for very long periods.
Yes?
Yes.
I remember that they were getting together in Friday evenings.
And they were preparing these big tables with lot of goodies on, on them with this hard boil eggs with black pepper with these salads with a number of dishes.
My grandmother was an exquisite master of the gastronomic art of cooking.
And--
Her, her name was grandma-- Margarita Cohen.
But they used to call her butcher, Grandma [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know why.
Butcher?
No butcher, [INAUDIBLE].
This obviously was some name, nickname of her that she was called when she was a young girl herself.
So she was very happy, obviously, to be called like that.
And they used to call her.
And she was one exquisite master of the art of cooking, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] Of course, I didn't had too long time to enjoy her mastery.
But returning back to these evenings when they were getting-- they were drinking this kind of liquor of-- with very little alcohol.
Of course, red wine was there, red wine.
And [INAUDIBLE]-- I will not forget this kind of liquor that become white when you pour a little bit of water in it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were singing songs, especially my uncle, brother of my mother.
His name was Uncle Raphael, or Uncle Gabriel, how I-- the children used to call him.
He was really something very special.
He was a conductor.
He's a professional-- was, he was businessmen, basically.
But he was writing poetry.
He was conducting choruses, amateur choruses sometime.
Sometime once, he [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] conducted one choir of-- based on the one oratory, oratory of Haydn, with amateur singers.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Of course, very soon after that, he disappeared appear, together with number of other my cousins.
Well, first of all, what happened was that in-- little bit after the year of 1940, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the thing start to drastically change.

Actually, before-- little before that, all the time-- any time when I was descending to play in the garden with the other boys from the neighborhood there, I felt already different attitudes toward me.
By other little bit older boys, even-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never will forget when I was-- I went there and was one summer day.
I don't recall when was '39 or till '40.
I was playing there.
And one of the older boy was looking me and watching and starting to throw, first of all, sand toward me, until he started to throw sand over my eyes.
And then I start to cry.
And he screamed, you dirty Jew.
Why don't you go to your own country, or your own place?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was perhaps three and a half or four years old.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

These were, were kind of first very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if it's possible to say slight symptoms there of new, new climates that were arriving the time in Bulgaria until one day we had to, to pack our-- some of our clothing and leave our home.
First of all, we had to move out of our home, going in different part of the city, not very far from the synagogue.
But this was for very short period of time.
This was some people that deliver some mail, some letter personally.
This was obviously some kind of ultimatum, not a friendly letter that's what we were supposed to do.
It already is not desirable Jewish people to live in this area.
There were kind of-- all the conversations, of course, everything start to, to have completely different tones, completely different character.

I felt-- of course, I was quite young in the time, that they were speaking about one or other Jew being beaten or been done something in his store or his business, and that they suspected some changes to happen without saying exactly what.
And this-- what happened, actually, was something that was expected, obviously, to happen that we have to move our-- out of our home.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- much later on, I understood actually what really what happening and, and how the, the society, the community of the capital, actually was moving and how, little by little, the government was absorb of the fascist elements that took totally the, the power, political power, in their hands.
The minister became, if I recall the name, Bogdan Filov, one very power, very strong anti-Semite.
Actually, I should say that basically, the attitude of the, the Bulgarian people were pretty sympathetic toward the Jewish people.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] those people that were in power, they wanted very much to please to their much more stronger, more powerful allies, which were the German.
Bulgaria was basically Germanophile country, country that were quite strongly attached with Germany from a much earlier period of time.
Matter of fact, the king of Bulgaria was descending from the German Coburg dynasty there.
Was a very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] complex situation, because Bulgarian people never stop to-- they are very warm and not only sympathetic, but a feeling of admiration for the Russian people.
Because were the Russian that saved them from the Turkish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yoke that they were for almost 500 years.
So this was also projected in, in the behave there in the capital during this period.

From one side, one kind of silent sympathy, but outspoken, very hostile officially feeling, until we had really to leave our beautiful home and move to one of the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] apartments of the area where they were concentrating Jewish people.
Was kind of poor area of the city.
This word ghetto, actually, I didn't hear there in, in-- but perhaps, this was the right word for.
Because there was only strictly Jewish people living there.
Was known-- this area was not very far from the synagogue, by the way.
And there was the-- they used to call the gypsy quarters, the quarters where the gypsy, gypsies were basically living.
Was only-- I was the, the youngest member and the only member, my, my mother and my father.
This was somewhere [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the early '40s.
I would like to say in early '40s-- '40, exactly '40, 1940-- not '40s-- actually '40.
Because we didn't stay there too long.
We stay there less than year.
In this place, yes, in these quarters.
After that, we had to pack meager belongings and take the train for the location that was indicated, again via this mail that they receive to [INAUDIBLE].
OK.
